description: The City of Light captivates with world-class art, iconic architecture, and exquisite cuisine. From the Eiffel Tower to charming cafe-lined boulevards, Paris defines romance and culture.
A vibrant Mediterranean gem where Gaudi's fantastical architecture meets golden beaches and world-class nightlife. Barcelona pulses with creative energy, tapas culture, and endless sunshine.
The Eternal City layers millennia of history beneath vibrant modern life. Ancient ruins, Renaissance art, and sublime Italian cuisine create an unmatched cultural experience.
A city of canals, cycling, and creative spirit. Amsterdam blends world-class museums, coffeehouse culture, and a famously open-minded atmosphere into one of Europe's most charming capitals.
A fairy-tale city of Gothic spires, Baroque palaces, and cobblestone streets. Prague offers incredible history and vibrant nightlife at remarkably affordable prices.
Iconic white-washed buildings cascade down volcanic cliffs above the deepest blue Aegean waters. Santorini is the ultimate romantic escape with breathtaking sunsets and Mediterranean cuisine.
Gateway to otherworldly landscapes of glaciers, geysers, and the Northern Lights. Reykjavik combines raw natural beauty with a surprisingly vibrant arts and music scene.
Sun-drenched hills overlooking the Tagus River, adorned with colorful tiles and the melancholic sounds of Fado music. Lisbon delivers old-world charm with a modern creative edge.
The Pearl of the Adriatic enchants with medieval walls, terracotta rooftops, and crystal-clear waters. Walk the same ancient streets that doubled as King's Landing in Game of Thrones.
A dramatic city of volcanic crags, medieval Old Town, and Georgian New Town elegance. Edinburgh blends Scottish history with world-famous festivals and a thriving food scene.
Imperial grandeur meets coffeehouse culture in this elegant capital. Vienna dazzles with palatial architecture, classical music heritage, and some of the finest pastries on Earth.
The happiest city in the world charms with colorful Nyhavn harbor, innovative Nordic cuisine, and world-class design. Copenhagen embodies Scandinavian cool and sustainable living.
Where East meets West across the Bosphorus strait. Istanbul enchants with the Blue Mosque, Grand Bazaar, and a culinary tradition that spans Ottoman, Mediterranean, and Middle Eastern flavors.
Nestled between two glacial lakes beneath the snow-capped Jungfrau massif. Interlaken is the adventure capital of Europe, offering paragliding, skiing, and jaw-dropping Alpine scenery.
Pastel villages cling to dramatic cliffs above the azure Mediterranean. The Amalfi Coast is a sun-soaked paradise of lemon groves, winding coastal roads, and sublime Italian cuisine.

An electrifying fusion of ultramodern technology and ancient tradition. Tokyo dazzles with neon-lit streets, Michelin-starred ramen shops, serene temples, and the world's most efficient transit system.
A tropical paradise of terraced rice paddies, ancient temples, and world-class surf breaks. Bali blends spiritual Balinese culture with affordable luxury and breathtaking natural beauty.
A sensory overload of golden temples, bustling night markets, and legendary street food. Bangkok is Southeast Asia's most dynamic city, where ancient traditions thrive alongside modern skylines.
Where K-pop meets Confucian palaces. Seoul buzzes with cutting-edge technology, incredible Korean BBQ, vibrant nightlife districts, and a beauty and fashion scene that sets global trends.
A captivating blend of French colonial elegance and Vietnamese hustle. Hanoi enchants with its Old Quarter's labyrinthine streets, legendary pho, and serene Hoan Kiem Lake.
A futuristic city-state of stunning architecture, lush gardens, and the world's best street food scene. Singapore seamlessly blends Chinese, Malay, Indian, and Western cultures.
Japan's cultural heart, where thousands of ancient temples and shrines nestle among bamboo groves and cherry blossoms. Kyoto preserves the grace of traditional Japanese aesthetics.
Crystal-clear turquoise waters lap against pristine white-sand beaches in this Indian Ocean paradise. The Maldives offers unparalleled luxury, world-class diving, and ultimate tropical escape.
A city of superlatives rising from the desert — tallest building, largest mall, most extravagant hotels. Dubai offers futuristic architecture, luxury shopping, and year-round sunshine.
India's beach paradise blends golden sands with Portuguese colonial heritage. Goa offers everything from tranquil yoga retreats and spice plantations to lively beach parties and seafood feasts.
Gateway to the Himalayas and a spiritual crossroads of Hindu and Buddhist traditions. Kathmandu's ancient temples, vibrant markets, and mountain vistas create an unforgettable experience.
A laid-back northern Thai city surrounded by misty mountains and over 300 ancient temples. Chiang Mai draws digital nomads, foodies, and wellness seekers with its low cost and high quality of life.
A sensory whirlwind of spice-scented souks, ornate palaces, and the call to prayer echoing over terracotta rooftops. Marrakech transports you to a world of North African magic and hospitality.
Where Table Mountain meets the Atlantic Ocean. Cape Town stuns with dramatic landscapes, world-class wineries, vibrant culture, and some of the continent's best beaches and cuisine.
Spice Island paradise with turquoise waters, white-sand beaches, and the historic Stone Town labyrinth. Zanzibar blends Swahili, Arab, and Indian influences into a tropical cultural mosaic.
The safari capital of the world, where you can spot lions in a national park within city limits. Nairobi is East Africa's vibrant hub for wildlife, culture, and innovation.
Stand before the last surviving Wonder of the Ancient World. Cairo's pyramids, the Sphinx, and the Egyptian Museum house treasures spanning five millennia of civilization.
Home to the thundering Victoria Falls, one of the Seven Natural Wonders of the World. Livingstone offers heart-pounding adventure with bungee jumping, white-water rafting, and safari experiences.
The city that never sleeps pulses with Broadway shows, world-class museums, iconic skylines, and a food scene representing every cuisine on Earth. New York is energy personified.
Caribbean turquoise waters meet ancient Mayan ruins. Cancun delivers world-class beaches, vibrant nightlife, and easy access to cenotes, jungle adventures, and archaeological wonders.
Where mountains meet the Pacific in one of the world's most livable cities. Vancouver offers skiing, beaches, rainforest trails, and an incredible Asian-fusion food scene — all within minutes.
Frozen in time with pastel-colored colonial buildings and 1950s American cars. Havana pulses with salsa music, rum, revolutionary history, and genuine warmth that defines Cuban culture.
Gateway to one of the planet's most biodiverse countries. Costa Rica offers cloud forests, volcanic hot springs, zip-lining through jungle canopies, and a commitment to eco-tourism.
Turquoise glacial lakes and towering Rocky Mountain peaks create one of the most photographed landscapes on Earth. Banff is a year-round paradise for hikers, skiers, and nature lovers.
One of the world's great megacities, bursting with Aztec history, Diego Rivera murals, and a food scene that rivals any global capital. Mexico City is culture, chaos, and charm in equal measure.
Bohemian-chic beach town where ancient Mayan clifftop ruins overlook the Caribbean Sea. Tulum attracts yoga practitioners, eco-conscious travelers, and anyone seeking tropical serenity.
The Paris of South America seduces with passionate tango, sizzling steakhouses, and vibrant neighborhoods. Buenos Aires is a cultural powerhouse with European elegance and Latin American fire.
Christ the Redeemer watches over golden beaches, lush mountains, and a city that lives for samba, Carnival, and the joy of the carioca lifestyle. Rio is beauty, rhythm, and pure energy.
The City of Eternal Spring has transformed from troubled past to innovative future. Medellin delights with perfect weather year-round, vibrant street art, and warm paisa hospitality.

Ancient Inca capital perched high in the Andes, gateway to the legendary Machu Picchu. Cusco's cobblestone streets, Spanish colonial churches, and living indigenous culture create a profound travel experience.
A Caribbean jewel of colorful colonial architecture, bougainvillea-draped balconies, and pulsing Afro-Caribbean rhythms. Cartagena's old walled city is a UNESCO World Heritage treasure.
Gateway to the awe-inspiring Perito Moreno Glacier and Los Glaciares National Park. El Calafate offers raw Patagonian beauty with turquoise lakes, rugged mountains, and pristine wilderness.
A high-altitude capital buzzing with street art, craft beer, and world-class museums. Bogota's La Candelaria neighborhood and surrounding Andean landscapes reveal Colombia's creative renaissance.
Harbor city icon defined by the Opera House, the Harbour Bridge, and golden surf beaches. Sydney delivers outdoor lifestyle, multicultural cuisine, and a relaxed cosmopolitan energy.
The adventure capital of the world, cradled by the Remarkables mountain range and Lake Wakatipu. Queenstown is where bungee jumping was born and every adrenaline sport thrives.
Gateway to Fiji's 333 islands of palm-fringed beaches, coral reefs, and warm Fijian hospitality. Nadi opens the door to one of the South Pacific's most welcoming tropical paradises.
Australia's cultural capital bursts with street art laneways, world-class coffee, live music, and sporting events. Melbourne is consistently ranked among the world's most livable cities.
The most romantic island on Earth, where overwater bungalows float above impossibly clear lagoons. Bora Bora's volcanic peaks and coral reefs create the ultimate luxury tropical escape.
The City of Sails stretches between two harbors, with volcanic cones, black-sand beaches, and world-class wine regions just minutes away. Auckland is New Zealand's multicultural gateway.
Tasmania's charming capital sits beneath the dramatic kunanyi/Mount Wellington. Hobart offers pristine wilderness, farm-to-table cuisine, MONA's provocative art, and some of Australia's best whisky.

A dazzling vertical city where neon-lit skyscrapers tower over ancient temples and bustling street markets. Hong Kong delivers world-class dim sum, legendary harbour views, and an electric urban energy that never sleeps.

China's imperial capital blends millennia of dynastic grandeur with cutting-edge modernity. Walk the Great Wall, explore the Forbidden City, and dive into Peking duck culture in a metropolis that has shaped civilisation for centuries.

The futuristic Pudong skyline meets colonial Bund architecture along the Huangpu River. Shanghai pulses with haute cuisine, art deco glamour, and a fashion-forward creativity that makes it Asia's most cosmopolitan megacity.

A food lover's paradise where legendary night markets serve up some of the world's best street eats. Taipei melds buzzing urban culture, serene mountain trails, and cutting-edge design with a uniquely warm hospitality.

Gateway to the majestic Angkor Wat temple complex, a wonder of the ancient world reclaimed by jungle. Siem Reap pairs profound Khmer heritage with vibrant night markets and astonishingly affordable luxury.

A UNESCO-listed jewel where saffron-robed monks collect alms at dawn along the Mekong. Luang Prabang enchants with gilded temples, cascading waterfalls, and a serenity that feels like time standing still.

The Pink City dazzles with rose-hued palaces, vibrant bazaars, and a Rajasthani grandeur that transports you to the age of maharajas. Jaipur's hilltop forts and glittering jewel trade make it India's most colourful destination.

Kerala's emerald gateway where Chinese fishing nets line the shore and backwater canals wind through coconut groves. Kochi layers Portuguese churches, Jewish synagogues, and spice-scented streets into an intoxicating cultural mosaic.

Sri Lanka's vibrant capital mixes colonial grandeur with modern energy, serving fiery curries beside turquoise Indian Ocean waves. Colombo is the launchpad for ancient ruins, misty tea plantations, and golden tropical beaches.

Japan's kitchen serves up legendary street food from takoyaki to okonomiyaki in neon-drenched alleyways. Osaka pairs boisterous Kansai humour with a magnificent castle, vibrant nightlife, and a culinary scene that rivals any city on Earth.

The rose-red city carved into sheer sandstone cliffs over two thousand years ago remains one of humanity's most breathtaking achievements. Walking through the narrow Siq to glimpse the Treasury at sunrise is a once-in-a-lifetime moment.

A sun-drenched Mediterranean city pulsing with start-up energy, Bauhaus architecture, and golden beaches. Tel Aviv's electric nightlife, gourmet food scene, and liberal culture make it the Middle East's most dynamic destination.

Oman's regal capital nestles between rugged Hajar mountains and a glittering Arabian Sea coastline. Muscat blends ancient forts, the stunning Grand Mosque, and pristine wadis with a gracious hospitality untouched by mass tourism.

The world's only carbon-negative country hides a capital of fortress monasteries, prayer-flag-draped hills, and streets without traffic lights. Thimphu offers a rare window into Gross National Happiness and Himalayan Buddhist culture.

Gateway to Mount Kinabalu and the coral paradise of Tunku Abdul Rahman Marine Park. Kota Kinabalu serves fiery Borneo seafood at waterfront sunsets while orangutans swing through nearby rainforest canopies.

Thailand's largest island fringes the Andaman Sea with powdery white beaches and turquoise lagoons. Phuket balances raucous Patong nightlife with serene hilltop temples and some of Southeast Asia's finest diving.

Central Vietnam's coastal jewel lines a crescent of golden sand between lush Marble Mountains and the emerald Son Tra peninsula. Da Nang delivers world-class bridges, phenomenal pho, and the gateway to ancient Hoi An.

The roof of the world unfolds around the iconic Potala Palace perched high on the Tibetan Plateau. Lhasa immerses you in Tibetan Buddhist monasteries, yak-butter tea rituals, and Himalayan panoramas at breathtaking 3,650 metres.

Mongolia's rugged capital is the gateway to endless steppes, eagle hunters, and nomadic ger camps under enormous starlit skies. Ulaanbaatar blends Soviet-era grit with a rising arts scene and the spirit of Genghis Khan.

The jewel of the Silk Road gleams with turquoise-tiled madrasas and the soaring Registan Square. Samarkand whispers of Tamerlane's empire while fragrant plov simmers in bazaar cauldrons, connecting East and West as it has for millennia.

The Land of Fire's capital juxtaposes a medieval walled old town with the futuristic Flame Towers blazing over the Caspian Sea. Baku surprises with lavish oil-boom architecture, hearty Caucasian cuisine, and a remarkably cosmopolitan energy.

Georgia's enchanting capital drapes across steep gorge walls, layering ancient churches, sulphur bathhouses, and a booming natural-wine scene. Tbilisi's legendary hospitality, vibrant nightlife, and khachapuri make it the Caucasus' hottest destination.

Saigon roars with motorbike rivers, French-colonial facades, and some of the planet's most thrilling street food. Ho Chi Minh City rewards explorers with war-history museums, rooftop cocktail bars, and a relentless creative hustle.

Cambodia's riverside capital blends glittering royal pagodas with sobering Khmer Rouge history and a fast-rising food and art scene. Phnom Penh's waterfront sunsets and buzzing night markets reveal a city reborn.

The golden Shwedagon Pagoda crowns a city of crumbling colonial charm, sizzling tea-leaf salads, and devout Buddhist devotion. Yangon opens the door to Myanmar's temples, floating villages, and landscapes untouched by mass tourism.

The Petronas Twin Towers soar above a jungle of Malay, Chinese, and Indian flavours fused into one of Asia's greatest food cities. Kuala Lumpur dazzles with mega-malls, street-food hawker stalls, and lush tropical parks.

Indonesia's sprawling megacity pulsates with traffic, rooftop bars, and an emerging arts scene built on centuries of Javanese, Betawi, and Dutch heritage. Jakarta rewards the curious with bold street food, atmospheric old-town quarters, and genuine urban adventure.

A chaotic, warm-hearted metropolis where Spanish-colonial Intramuros meets glittering Makati skyscrapers. Manila packs electrifying nightlife, Filipino comfort food, and the gateway to 7,641 tropical islands into one irrepressible city.

The world's oldest continuously inhabited city burns eternal on the banks of the Ganges. Varanasi's dawn boat rides past cremation ghats, chanting priests, and swirling marigolds deliver Hinduism's most profound spiritual encounter.

The City of Lakes floats whitewashed palaces on mirror-still waters framed by the Aravalli Hills. Udaipur is India at its most romantic — think rooftop dinners, boat rides to island palaces, and Rajput heritage dripping in opulence.

India's maximum city slams Bollywood glamour against crashing Arabian Sea waves and the colonial grandeur of the Gateway of India. Mumbai's legendary street food, pulsing nightlife, and sheer human energy make it utterly unforgettable.

Seven ancient cities layered beneath a roaring modern capital of Mughal tombs, Sikh temples, and aromatic spice markets. Delhi's street food alone — from butter chicken to chaat — justifies the sensory overload of this incredible metropolis.

Kazakhstan's former capital sprawls beneath the snow-capped Tien Shan mountains, offering alpine skiing, green bazaars bursting with dried fruit, and a surprisingly vibrant café culture. Almaty is Central Asia's most liveable and underrated city.

A leafy Soviet-era capital at the foot of the Ala-Too mountains and the gateway to high-altitude yurt treks around epic Issyk-Kul lake. Bishkek surprises with craft-beer bars, bustling Osh Bazaar, and jaw-dropping alpine wilderness.

One of the Silk Road's holiest cities preserves 140 ancient monuments beneath turquoise domes and soaring minarets. Bukhara's labyrinthine old town, hammam baths, and centuries-old trading domes transport you to a merchant past.

The yoga capital of the world sits where the Ganges tumbles out of the Himalayas in emerald rapids. Rishikesh draws seekers with ashram stays, whitewater rafting, and the same spiritual magnetism that inspired the Beatles.

A lantern-lit ancient trading port where tailor shops line honey-coloured streets and the Thu Bon River glows at dusk. Hoi An enchants with custom-made fashion, extraordinary banh mi, and rice-paddy cycling under coconut palms.

Hokkaido's winter wonderland serves legendary miso ramen, fresh seafood, and world-class powder skiing beneath frosted birch forests. In summer, Sapporo transforms with lavender fields, beer gardens, and cool mountain air.

A volcanic island paradise ringed by tangerine groves, black-lava beaches, and the famous haenyeo sea-women divers. Jeju blends dramatic lava tubes, Korea's tallest peak, and honeymoon resorts into a subtropical escape.

A duty-free archipelago of 99 islands draped in jungle, eagle-filled skies, and emerald waters. Langkawi's SkyBridge, mangrove kayaking, and pristine beaches make it Malaysia's ultimate island retreat.

A UNESCO-listed street-art haven and Southeast Asia's undisputed hawker-food champion. Penang's George Town layers Chinese shophouses, Indian temples, and Malay kampungs into a spice-scented cultural kaleidoscope.
The Queen City of the South pairs Spanish-colonial basilicas with world-famous whale-shark snorkelling and powder-white Bantayan beaches. Cebu's lechon is a Filipino food legend worth the trip alone.

Towering limestone karsts erupt from crystal-clear lagoons in what many call the world's most beautiful island scenery. El Nido is a bucket-list paradise for island-hopping, snorkelling, and watching sunsets melt into the South China Sea.

Bali's chic beach-club capital where trendsetting boutiques and sunset cocktail bars line a golden stretch of Indian Ocean sand. Seminyak delivers designer spas, world-class dining, and Bali's most fashionable nightlife.

Bali's cultural heart nestles among emerald rice terraces, sacred monkey forests, and ancient Hindu temples. Ubud pulses with yoga retreats, Balinese dance performances, and artisan workshops in a lush jungle setting.

A sweeping crescent bay lined with coconut palms and turquoise waters dotted with offshore islands. Nha Trang mixes Vietnamese seafood feasts, lively beachside nightlife, and some of the country's best diving and snorkelling.

Thailand's original royal beach resort town blends long golden sands with charming night markets and world-class golf courses. Hua Hin offers a sophisticated coastal retreat just hours from Bangkok, without the party-island crowds.

Dramatic limestone pinnacles jut from emerald Andaman waters around Railay Beach and the Phi Phi Islands. Krabi delivers rock climbing, kayaking through mangrove forests, and some of Thailand's most photogenic coastline.

Southeast Asia's most laid-back capital drowses along the Mekong, mixing French-colonial bakeries with gilded Buddhist stupas. Vientiane's sleepy charm, affordable massage parlours, and riverside sunsets offer a blissful antidote to city stress.

Jordan's hilly capital cascades in white limestone across seven jabals, threading Roman ruins, aromatic souks, and the warmest Arab hospitality. Amman is the springboard for Petra and the Dead Sea, with a buzzing café culture all its own.

Sacred to three faiths, Jerusalem's golden-stoned Old City radiates millennia of devotion from the Western Wall to the Church of the Holy Sepulchre to the Dome of the Rock. Few places on Earth concentrate more history per cobblestone.

The Norway of Arabia slices dramatic fjords into the Strait of Hormuz, where dolphins leap past traditional dhow boats. Musandam offers world-class diving, sheer cliff snorkelling, and an isolated splendour far from the tourist trail.

Surreal boulder-strewn landscapes cradle the ruins of the mighty Vijayanagara Empire across an otherworldly terrain. Hampi's 500-year-old temples, elephant stables, and sunset viewpoints create one of India's most unforgettable UNESCO sites.

A high-altitude desert of stark moonscapes, turquoise lakes, and centuries-old Buddhist monasteries perched on impossible ridges. Ladakh's Pangong Lake and Nubra Valley deliver some of the most dramatic landscapes on the planet.

Annapurna's snow peaks reflect in the tranquil waters of Phewa Lake in Nepal's adventure capital. Pokhara pairs world-famous trekking, paragliding over Himalayan ridges, and lakeside chill vibes at astonishingly low prices.

A wind-swept Atlantic fortress town where blue fishing boats bob beneath rampart walls and Jimi Hendrix legends linger. Essaouira's medina mixes surf culture, fresh-grilled sardines, and bohemian gallery charm in a laid-back Moroccan escape.

The world's largest car-free urban zone conceals 9,000 labyrinthine alleyways of medieval markets, leather tanneries, and the oldest university on Earth. Fes is Morocco's spiritual and intellectual heart, raw and utterly captivating.

Ghana's sun-drenched capital pulses with Afrobeats, vibrant kente cloth markets, and some of West Africa's most exciting street food. Accra pairs sobering slave-trade history with a joyful contemporary art and music scene.

Perched on Africa's westernmost tip, Dakar vibrates with mbalax rhythms, technicolour pirogues, and the poignant history of Gorée Island. Senegal's capital serves legendary thieboudienne while a thriving fashion and art scene reshapes the continent.

Africa's cleanest and safest capital rises across a thousand emerald hills, balancing painful genocide remembrance with remarkable forward-looking optimism. Kigali is the gateway to life-changing mountain gorilla trekking in the Virungas.

The safari capital of East Africa sits beneath the snow-dusted peak of Mount Meru, launching expeditions to the Serengeti's Great Migration and the Ngorongoro Crater. Arusha buzzes with explorer energy and Maasai cultural encounters.

A tropical paradise where turquoise lagoons meet volcanic peaks and Creole, Indian, Chinese, and French cultures fuse into one of the world's most harmonious island nations. Mauritius delivers luxury resorts, underwater waterfalls, and rum-soaked sunsets.

Granite boulders frame powder-white beaches lapped by water so clear it barely seems real. Seychelles' main island pairs luxury eco-resorts, Creole cuisine, and jungle-clad peaks with some of the Indian Ocean's most pristine coral reefs.

Madagascar's hilltop capital, known affectionately as Tana, gates an island of lemurs, baobab avenues, and biodiversity found nowhere else on Earth. Antananarivo charms with Malagasy markets, royal palaces, and a wonderfully unique cultural identity.

Africa's diplomatic capital perches at 2,400 metres, serving the world's finest coffee in the land where it was born. Addis Ababa mixes ancient Aksumite heritage, the Ethiopian Orthodox church's mystical rituals, and an exhilarating jazz scene.

Namibia's clean, compact capital stands as the gateway to Sossusvlei's towering red dunes, Etosha's wildlife paradise, and the haunting Skeleton Coast. Windhoek itself surprises with German-colonial architecture, craft breweries, and wide African skies.

Africa's most expensive city sprawls along the Atlantic with a contrast of oil-wealth towers and vibrant musseques pulsing with kizomba and semba rhythms. Luanda rewards adventurous travellers with raw energy and untouristed African beaches.

Portuguese-colonial architecture, fiery peri-peri prawns, and a vibrant Afro-jazz scene define Mozambique's laid-back Indian Ocean capital. Maputo serves as the gateway to the pristine Bazaruto Archipelago and its whale-shark waters.

A whitewashed medina stacked above ancient Carthage and turquoise Mediterranean coves. Tunis layers Phoenician ruins, Ottoman palaces, and French art-deco cafés into North Africa's most underrated cultural capital.

The world's greatest open-air museum lines both banks of the Nile with the Valley of the Kings, Karnak Temple, and 3,500 years of pharaonic wonder. Luxor delivers bucket-list moments from dawn hot-air balloon rides over ancient tombs.

Morocco's economic powerhouse blends the jaw-dropping Hassan II Mosque rising from the Atlantic with art-deco boulevards and a modern culinary renaissance. Casablanca is grittier and more authentic than its cinematic legend suggests.

The Blue Pearl of Morocco drapes every wall, staircase, and doorway in hypnotic shades of cobalt and azure against the Rif Mountains. Chefchaouen is a photographer's dream of winding blue alleys, artisan crafts, and mountain serenity.

A sun-baked Mediterranean island where whitewashed villages, olive groves, and Africa's oldest synagogue float in warm turquoise shallows. Djerba blends Berber heritage, beach lounging, and atmospheric souks at a blissfully slow pace.

Uganda's seven-hilled capital buzzes with boda-boda energy, rolex street-food wraps, and a thriving nightlife scene. Kampala launches epic journeys to gorilla trekking, Nile rapids at Jinja, and the snow-capped Rwenzori Mountains.

Zimbabwe's highland capital offers wide boulevards, balancing rocks, and a resilient creative spirit expressed through vibrant stone sculpture and a growing foodie scene. Harare is the unexpected gateway to Great Zimbabwe and Eastern Highland forests.

South Africa's subtropical surf city lines the warm Indian Ocean with a Golden Mile promenade, Zulu heritage, and the largest Indian diaspora community outside Asia. Durban delivers bunny chow, shark-diving, and year-round beach weather.

The gateway to Kruger National Park and the Panorama Route's jaw-dropping Blyde River Canyon. Nelspruit launches Big Five safaris through over 19,000 square kilometres of pristine African bushveld teeming with wildlife.

East Africa's oldest Swahili settlement drifts through car-free coral-stone alleys where dhows sail past centuries-old mosques. Lamu's donkey transport, mangrove-fringed beaches, and Swahili poetry festivals make it a living cultural treasure.

Zanzibar's UNESCO-listed heart weaves a labyrinth of carved doorways, spice bazaars, and rooftop restaurants overlooking the Indian Ocean. Stone Town's Omani, Indian, and African heritage creates a cultural fusion as intoxicating as its clove-scented air.

The Smoke That Thunders plummets over a mile-wide basalt curtain in one of the planet's most awe-inspiring natural spectacles. Victoria Falls pairs the Devil's Pool, bungee-jumping, and Zambezi sunset cruises into Africa's ultimate adventure destination.

West Africa's cultural capital hosts the continent's premier film festival (FESPACO) and vibrates with live music, moto taxis, and an irrepressible creative spirit. Ouaga rewards intrepid travellers with genuine hospitality and authentic Sahelian culture.

Senegal's crumbling colonial gem straddles the Senegal River on a narrow island of faded French grandeur and West African soul. Saint-Louis enchants with its jazz festival, pelican-filled Djoudj wetlands, and haunting Saharan-edge atmosphere.

North Africa's highest peak towers at 4,167 metres above Berber villages clinging to the High Atlas slopes. Toubkal trekking delivers mountain refuges, walnut groves, and panoramic Saharan views that stretch to eternity.

A palm-fringed ribbon of white coral sand lapped by warm turquoise Indian Ocean waters, consistently ranked among Africa's finest beaches. Diani pairs snorkelling with colobus monkeys overhead and Maasai warriors on the sand at sunset.

Kenya's sultry coastal city wraps a centuries-old Swahili port in coral-block Old Town alleys, Fort Jesus, and the iconic elephant tusks of Moi Avenue. Mombasa serves gateway access to Kenya's south coast beaches and the Shimba Hills.

A hilly, fog-kissed metropolis where the Golden Gate Bridge frames a city famed for tech innovation, world-class dining, and vibrant counter-culture. From Chinatown to the Castro, each neighborhood pulses with its own creative energy.

The sprawling City of Angels stretches from sun-drenched Pacific beaches to the Hollywood Hills. LA delivers star-studded glamour, a booming food scene, and year-round sunshine that fuels an outdoor lifestyle.

Where Art Deco architecture meets turquoise waters and a pulsating Latin beat. Miami's South Beach, Little Havana, and Wynwood Walls create a tropical playground for sun-seekers and nightlife lovers alike.

The Live Music Capital of the World serves up a mouthwatering barbecue scene, a thriving tech culture, and quirky independent spirit. Austin's legendary Sixth Street and beautiful Barton Springs keep visitors coming back.

The Windy City rises boldly from the shores of Lake Michigan with iconic architecture, deep-dish pizza, and world-class museums. Chicago's blues heritage and electric neighborhoods make it one of America's great cultural centers.

A sultry, soulful city where jazz spills from every doorway and Creole aromas fill the French Quarter. New Orleans celebrates life with Mardi Gras abandon, legendary po'boys, and a spirit found nowhere else on Earth.

Music City lives up to its name with honky-tonks lining Broadway, the Grand Ole Opry, and a booming culinary and craft cocktail scene. Nashville blends Southern charm with a youthful creative renaissance.

Pastel-colored colonial buildings line the harbourfront of this mid-Atlantic island paradise. Bermuda offers pink-sand beaches, world-class diving on historic shipwrecks, and a sophisticated blend of British and Caribbean culture.

The birthplace of reggae and ska pulses with an irrepressible creative energy rooted in the Blue Mountains' shadow. Kingston delivers the Bob Marley Museum, jerk chicken shacks, and an authentic Caribbean experience far from resort crowds.

Centuries-old Spanish colonial fortresses guard a city of cobblestone streets, rainbow-painted houses, and sizzling nightlife. Old San Juan's charm pairs perfectly with nearby bioluminescent bays and El Yunque rainforest.

A bilingual metropolis where French joie de vivre meets North American energy. Montreal enchants with cobblestoned Old Port, legendary bagels, world-famous jazz and comedy festivals, and a year-round underground city.

North America's most European city enchants with its UNESCO-listed walled Old Town, the fairy-tale Château Frontenac, and a revered Winter Carnival. French-Canadian cuisine and a magical snowy cityscape make it a four-season gem.

The aurora borealis capital of North America sits on the edge of Great Slave Lake in Canada's subarctic wilderness. Yellowknife offers dogsledding, ice roads, and some of the clearest Northern Lights viewing on the planet.

Crimson buttes and towering sandstone formations create a surreal desert cathedral renowned for its spiritual energy vortexes. Sedona draws hikers, artists, and wellness seekers to its red-rock trails and world-class spa retreats.

Maui's Valley Isle delivers everything from the otherworldly sunrise atop Haleakalā to the lush Road to Hana and pristine snorkeling at Molokini Crater. Whale-watching, luaus, and golden beaches define this Pacific paradise.

Alaska's largest city is the gateway to vast wilderness where glaciers calve into turquoise fjords and grizzlies fish for salmon. Anchorage offers midnight sun in summer, aurora viewing in winter, and unrivaled outdoor adventures year-round.

The nation's capital overflows with free world-class Smithsonian museums, iconic monuments, and cherry-blossom-lined boulevards. Washington DC blends political power with vibrant neighborhoods, craft breweries, and a diverse culinary scene.

America's walking city traces the birth of a nation along the Freedom Trail while ivy-league campuses fuel modern innovation. Boston pairs colonial charm with championship sports culture, legendary seafood, and a fiery autumn foliage backdrop.

Keep Portland Weird is more than a slogan in this Pacific Northwest haven of craft breweries, independent bookstores, and farm-to-table dining. Ringed by forests and snow-capped peaks, Portland celebrates creativity and sustainability.

The Emerald City pairs a legendary coffee culture and thriving tech scene with stunning views of Puget Sound and Mount Rainier. Pike Place Market, grunge heritage, and easy access to Pacific Northwest wilderness complete the picture.

A highland jewel of indigenous culture famed for mole sauces, mezcal, and one of the world's great Day of the Dead celebrations. Oaxaca's colonial centro, vibrant markets, and nearby Zapotec ruins of Monte Albán captivate every traveler.

A kaleidoscope of candy-colored houses tumbles down a ravine in one of Mexico's most photogenic colonial cities. Guanajuato enchants with underground streets, the macabre Mummy Museum, and the famous Cervantino arts festival.

Once a sleepy fishing village, Playa del Carmen now buzzes with beach clubs, cenote diving, and easy-going Caribbean vibes along pedestrian Quinta Avenida. It serves as the perfect base for Mayan ruins, snorkeling reefs, and island day-trips to Cozumel.

The colorful Bahamian capital pairs duty-free shopping on Bay Street with turquoise waters and the famous swimming pigs of the Exumas nearby. Nassau's Atlantis resort, colonial forts, and Junkanoo festival celebrate island life at its finest.

Ringed by three volcanoes, this UNESCO colonial treasure charms with crumbling baroque churches, cobblestone streets, and one of Central America's best Spanish-school scenes. Antigua's affordable cafés and vibrant Semana Santa processions are legendary.

The crown jewel of Honduras' Bay Islands sits atop the Mesoamerican Barrier Reef, offering some of the world's most affordable scuba diving. Roatan's laid-back vibe, mangrove kayaking, and warm Caribbean waters attract divers and beach bums alike.

Gateway to the world's second-largest barrier reef, Belize City launches adventures to the Great Blue Hole, jungle-cloaked Mayan temples, and pristine cayes. A Creole-Caribbean fusion of culture and cuisine permeates this coastal hub.

Host of the 2010 Winter Olympics, Whistler Blackcomb boasts North America's largest ski area with over 200 marked runs. In summer the peaks transform into a mountain-biking and hiking paradise surrounded by old-growth forest and alpine lakes.

One of the natural wonders of the world thunders with six million cubic feet of water plunging every minute. Beyond the iconic Horseshoe Falls, the region offers award-winning wineries, the scenic Niagara Parkway, and dazzling nighttime illuminations.

Antebellum mansions, Spanish-moss-draped oaks, and a culinary renaissance make Charleston one of the American South's most beguiling cities. The Holy City's pastel Rainbow Row, waterfront promenades, and Lowcountry cuisine earn perennial 'best city' accolades.

Twenty-two oak-canopied squares anchor one of the largest National Historic Landmark districts in the United States. Savannah beguiles with ghost tours, restored Victorian architecture, and a Southern-hospitality pace that invites long, leisurely stays.

America's oldest capital city radiates adobe warmth beneath turquoise skies at 7,000 feet. Santa Fe's world-renowned art market, Georgia O'Keeffe Museum, and ancient Pueblo heritage create an intoxicating blend of creativity and spirituality.

Waikiki's legendary crescent beach fronts a vibrant Pacific metropolis backed by the volcanic ridges of Diamond Head and the Ko'olau Range. Honolulu blends Hawaiian cultural traditions, world-class surfing, and Pearl Harbor remembrance into an unforgettable island capital.

A glittering Rocky Mountain resort town where champagne powder skiing meets summer music festivals and world-class hiking. Aspen's four mountains, farm-to-table dining scene, and celebrity-studded après-ski define luxury mountain living.

The Mile High City basks in 300 days of annual sunshine with the Rocky Mountain Front Range as a dramatic backdrop. Denver's craft-beer scene, Red Rocks Amphitheatre, and easy ski-trip access make it an outdoor-lover's urban basecamp.

Chile's cosmopolitan capital sits in a valley framed by the snow-capped Andes, offering world-class wine regions just an hour away. Santiago buzzes with innovative gastronomy, street art in Barrio Bellavista, and easy access to both ski slopes and Pacific beaches.

Chile's bohemian port city cascades down 42 cerros covered in street art, rickety ascensores, and rainbow-painted tin houses. Valparaíso's UNESCO heritage, thriving arts scene, and Pacific panoramas make it South America's most photogenic hillside canvas.

Uruguay's laid-back capital stretches along the Río de la Plata waterfront with a lovely Rambla promenade, Art Deco architecture, and legendary steak-and-Tannat culture. Montevideo's Mercado del Puerto and candombe drum traditions make it South America's hidden gem.

The world's highest official capital straddles the equator in a valley ringed by snow-topped volcanoes. Quito's UNESCO-listed colonial old town glows with gold-leaf churches, bustling plazas, and a crafts market that rivals any on the continent.

The world's highest administrative capital spills down a dramatic canyon beneath the triple-peaked Illimani. La Paz astounds with witches' markets, death-defying cable-car commutes, and the world's most dangerous road bike ride nearby.

Crowned the gastronomic capital of the Americas, Lima layers ancient Incan heritage with Spanish colonial grandeur and a coast-hugging metropolis. From ceviche temples and Miraflores cliffs to the catacombs of San Francisco, Lima is a feast for every sense.

The main hub of the Galápagos Islands puts visitors face-to-face with giant tortoises, marine iguanas, and blue-footed boobies found nowhere else on Earth. The Charles Darwin Research Station and pristine snorkeling sites make this a once-in-a-lifetime wildlife destination.

Deep in the Amazon basin, Manaus is the launchpad for river-lodge expeditions into the world's largest rainforest. The famed Meeting of the Waters, the opulent Teatro Amazonas opera house, and pink river dolphins define this jungle metropolis.

Argentina's Patagonian lake district channels alpine charm with chocolate shops, log-cabin breweries, and Nahuel Huapi Lake shimmering beneath volcanic peaks. Bariloche is both a premier ski destination and a summer trekking paradise.

An island city blessed with 42 beaches ranging from calm lagoons to world-class surf breaks. Florianópolis pairs Brazilian beach culture with fresh-oyster restaurants, sand-dune hikes, and a festive nightlife that peaks in Carnival season.

The vibrant heart of Afro-Brazilian culture pulses with capoeira circles, candomblé rituals, and the largest street Carnival on Earth. Salvador's Pelourinho district dazzles with pastel colonial facades, acarajé street food, and music on every corner.

Gateway to the world's largest salt flat, where a blinding white expanse stretches to the horizon and transforms into a giant mirror during the rainy season. The Salar de Uyuni's surreal landscapes include cactus islands, flamingo lagoons, and geysers at nearly 12,000 feet.

Billed as the southernmost city in the world, Ushuaia clings to the Beagle Channel beneath jagged Martial Mountains at the edge of Tierra del Fuego. It's the jumping-off point for Antarctic cruises and home to Patagonian hiking, penguin colonies, and the legendary End of the World train.

Argentina's wine capital sits at the foot of the Andes, producing world-famous Malbec across sun-drenched vineyards. Mendoza offers cycling wine tours, white-water rafting, Aconcagua trekking, and a food scene that rivals Buenos Aires.

South America's St-Tropez gleams with yacht-filled marinas, high-rise condos, and miles of pristine Atlantic beach. Punta del Este's glamorous summer season, celebrity art installations, and Casapueblo sunset rituals attract the continent's elite.

Peru's trekking capital nestles in the Callejón de Huaylas beneath the glaciated peaks of the Cordillera Blanca. Huaraz offers the legendary Santa Cruz trek, turquoise Llanganuco lakes, and some of the most dramatic Andean scenery on Earth.

The White City gleams with sillar volcanic-stone architecture beneath the perfectly conical El Misti volcano. Arequipa's Santa Catalina Monastery, the world's deepest Colca Canyon, and a celebrated picanterías food tradition rival anything in Lima.

Bolivia's constitutional capital and most beautiful city enchants with whitewashed colonial buildings, peaceful plazas, and a year-round spring-like climate. Sucre's dinosaur footprint park, chocolate factories, and indigenous textile markets offer quirky surprises at every turn.

A perfectly preserved 17th-century Portuguese colonial port wedged between the Atlantic and the jungle-clad Serra da Bocaina. Paraty's car-free cobblestone streets, cachaça distilleries, and emerald island-hopping make it one of Brazil's most romantic escapes.

Perched at 3,800 meters on the shore of Lake Titicaca, Puno is the gateway to the famous floating Uros reed islands and Taquile Island's ancient textile traditions. The city's Candelaria Festival is one of South America's most spectacular cultural celebrations.

The thundering curtain of Iguazú Falls — 275 cascades spanning nearly two miles — ranks among the planet's most awe-inspiring natural spectacles. Puerto Iguazú offers jungle walkways, speedboat rides into the Devil's Throat, and a tri-border meeting of Argentina, Brazil, and Paraguay.

The undisputed world capital of salsa dancing keeps the rhythm alive in packed dance clubs and open-air escuelas across the city. Cali's warm climate, affordable prices, and the annual Feria de Cali make it Colombia's most exuberant night out.

Colombia's Amazonian frontier town sits at the triple border with Brazil and Peru on the mighty Amazon River. Leticia offers jungle treks to indigenous communities, pink dolphin spotting, piranha fishing, and canopy walks deep in primary rainforest.

Brazil's ecotourism capital astounds with crystal-clear rivers where snorkelers float among tropical fish in visibility exceeding 40 meters. Bonito's limestone caves, macaw sinkholes, and sustainable tourism model make it a nature lover's dream in the heart of Mato Grosso do Sul.

A car-free tropical island draped in Atlantic Forest where hiking trails connect over 100 pristine beaches and secluded coves. Ilha Grande's Lopes Mendes beach, underwater shipwreck dives, and lazy hammock culture embody the ultimate Brazilian island escape.

The global center for banking and finance, Zurich lies at the north end of Lake Zurich in northern Switzerland. The picturesque lanes of the central Altstadt (Old Town), on either side of the Limmat River, reflect its pre-medieval history. Waterfront promenades like the Limmatquai follow the river toward the 17th-century Rathaus (town hall).

Munich, Bavaria’s capital, is home to centuries-old buildings and numerous museums. The city is known for its annual Oktoberfest celebration and its beer halls, including the famed Hofbräuhaus, founded in 1589. In the Altstadt (Old Town), central Marienplatz square contains landmarks such as the Neo-Gothic Neues Rathaus (town hall), with a popular glockenspiel show that chimes and reenacts stories from the 16th century.

Stockholm, the capital of Sweden, encompasses 14 islands and more than 50 bridges on an extensive Baltic Sea archipelago. The cobblestone streets and ochre-colored buildings of Gamla Stan (the old town) are home to the 13th-century Storkyrkan Cathedral, the Royal Palace and the Nobel Museum. Ferries and sightseeing boats shuttle passengers between the islands.

Dublin, capital of the Republic of Ireland, is on Ireland’s east coast at the mouth of the River Liffey. Its historic buildings include Dublin Castle, dating to the 13th century, and imposing St Patrick’s Cathedral, founded in 1191. City parks include landscaped St Stephen’s Green and huge Phoenix Park, containing Dublin Zoo. The National Museum of Ireland explores Irish heritage and culture.

Florence, capital of Italy’s Tuscany region, is home to many masterpieces of Renaissance art and architecture. One of its most iconic sights is the Duomo, a cathedral with a terracotta-tiled dome engineered by Brunelleschi and a bell tower by Giotto. The Galleria dell'Accademia displays Michelangelo’s “David” sculpture. The Uffizi Gallery exhibits Botticelli’s “The Birth of Venus” and da Vinci’s “Annunciation.”

Brussels, the capital of Belgium, is a major administrative center for the European Union. The city is known for its ornate Grand-Place, a UNESCO World Heritage site, surrounded by opulent guildhalls and the elegant Hôtel de Ville (Town Hall). It's also famous for its comic book culture, with murals of characters like Tintin adorning city walls. The city offers a rich culinary experience, from world-class chocolates and waffles to a wide variety of Belgian beers.

Madrid, Spain's central capital, is a city of elegant boulevards and expansive, manicured parks such as the Buen Retiro. It’s renowned for its rich repositories of European art, including the Prado Museum’s works by Goya, Velázquez and other Spanish masters. The heart of old Hapsburg Madrid is the portico-lined Plaza Mayor, and nearby is the baroque Royal Palace and Armory, displaying historic weaponry.

Berlin, Germany’s capital, dates to the 13th century. Reminders of the city's turbulent 20th-century history include its Holocaust memorial and the Berlin Wall's graffitied remains. Divided during the Cold War, its 18th-century Brandenburg Gate has become a symbol of reunification. The city's also known for its art scene and modern landmarks like the gold-colored, swoop-roofed Berliner Philharmonie, built in 1963.

Helsinki, Finland’s southern capital, sits on a peninsula in the Gulf of Finland. Its central avenue, Mannerheimintie, is flanked by institutions including the National Museum, tracing Finnish history from the Stone Age to the present. Also on Mannerheimintie are the imposing Parliament House and Kiasma, a contemporary art museum. Ornate red-brick Uspenski Cathedral overlooks a harbor.

Oslo, the capital of Norway, sits on the country’s southern coast at the head of the Oslofjord. It’s known for its green spaces and museums. Many of these are on the Bygdøy Peninsula, including the Norwegian Maritime Museum and the Viking Ship Museum, with Viking ships from the 9th century. The Holmenkollbakken is a ski-jumping hill with panoramic views of the fjord. It also has a ski museum.

Salzburg is an Austrian city on the border of Germany, with views of the Eastern Alps. The city is divided by the Salzach River, with medieval and baroque buildings of the pedestrian Altstadt (Old City) on its left bank, facing the 19th-century Neustadt (New City) on its right. The Altstadt is the birthplace of famed composer Mozart, preserving his childhood instruments. It's also the setting for the film 'The Sound of Music'.

Budapest, Hungary’s capital, is bisected by the River Danube. Its 19th-century Chain Bridge connects the hilly Buda district with flat Pest. A funicular runs up Castle Hill to Buda’s Old Town, where the Budapest History Museum traces city life from Roman times. Trinity Square is home to 13th-century Matthias Church and the turrets of the Fishermen’s Bastion, which offer sweeping views.

Kraków is a southern Poland city near the border of the Czech Republic. It's known for its well-preserved medieval core and Jewish quarter. Its old town – ringed by Planty Park and remnants of the city’s medieval walls – is centered on the stately, expansive Rynek Glówny (market square). This plaza is the site of the Cloth Hall, a Renaissance-era trading outpost, and St. Mary’s Basilica, a 14th-century Gothic church.

Athens is the capital of Greece. It was also at the heart of Ancient Greece, a powerful civilization and empire. The city is still dominated by 5th-century BC landmarks, including the Acropolis, a hilltop citadel topped with ancient buildings like the colonnaded Parthenon temple. The Acropolis Museum, along with the National Archaeological Museum, preserves sculptures, vases, jewelry and more from Ancient Greece.

Geneva is a city in Switzerland that lies at the southern tip of expansive Lac Léman (Lake Geneva). Surrounded by the Alps and Jura mountains, the city has views of dramatic Mont Blanc. Headquarters of Europe’s United Nations and the Red Cross, it’s a global hub for diplomacy and banking. French influence is widespread, from the language to gastronomy and bohemian districts like Carouge.

Nice, the capital of the Alpes-Maritimes department on the French Riviera, sits on the pebbly shores of the Baie des Anges. Founded by the Greeks and later a retreat for 19th-century European elite, the city has also long attracted artists. Former resident Henri Matisse is honored with a career-spanning collection of paintings at Musée Matisse. Musée Marc Chagall features some of his namesake's major religious works.

Marseille, a port city in southern France, has been a crossroads of immigration and trade since its founding by the Greeks circa 600 B.C. At its heart is the Vieux-Port (Old Port), where fishmongers sell their catch along the boat-lined quay. Basilique Notre-Dame-de-la-Garde is a Romanesque-Byzantine church. Modern landmarks include Le Corbusier’s influential Cité Radieuse complex and Zaha Hadid’s CMA CGM Tower.

Lyon, the capital city in France’s Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes region, sits at the junction of the Rhône and Saône rivers. Its center reflects 2,000 years of history from the Roman Amphithéâtre des Trois Gaules, medieval and Renaissance architecture in Vieux (Old) Lyon, to the modern Confluence district on the Presqu'île peninsula. Traboules, covered passageways between buildings, connect Vieux Lyon and La Croix-Rousse hill.

Bordeaux, hub of the famed wine-growing region, is a port city on the Garonne River in southwestern France. It’s known for its Gothic Cathédrale Saint-André, 18th- to 19th-century mansions and notable art museums such as the Musée des Beaux-Arts de Bordeaux. Public gardens line the curving river quays. The grand Place de la Bourse, centered on the Three Graces fountain, overlooks the Miroir d’Eau reflecting pool.

Strasbourg is the capital city of the Grand Est region, formerly Alsace, in northeastern France. It's also the formal seat of the European Parliament and sits near the German border, with culture and architecture blending German and French influences. Its Gothic Cathédrale Notre-Dame features daily shows from its astronomical clock and sweeping views of the Rhine River from partway up its 142m spire.

Manchester is a major city in the northwest of England with a rich industrial heritage. The Castlefield conservation area’s 18th-century canal system recalls the city’s days as a textile powerhouse, and visitors can trace this history at the interactive Museum of Science & Industry. The revitalised Salford Quays dockyards now house the Daniel Libeskind-designed Imperial War Museum North and The Lowry arts centre.

Liverpool is a maritime city in northwest England, where the River Mersey meets the Irish Sea. A key trade and migration port from the 18th to the early 20th centuries, it's also, famously, the hometown of The Beatles. Ferries cruise the waterfront, where the iconic mercantile buildings known as the 'Three Graces' – Royal Liver Building, Cunard Building and Port of Liverpool Building – stand on the Pier Head.

Glasgow is a port city on the River Clyde in Scotland's western Lowlands. It's famed for its Victorian and art nouveau architecture, a rich legacy of the city's 18th–20th-century prosperity due to trade and shipbuilding. Today it's a national cultural hub, home to institutions including the Scottish Opera, Scottish Ballet and National Theatre of Scotland, as well as acclaimed museums and a thriving music scene.

Bristol is a city straddling the River Avon in the southwest of England with a prosperous maritime history. Its former city-centre port is now a cultural hub, the Harbourside, where the M Shed museum explores local social and industrial heritage. The harbour's 19th-century warehouses now contain restaurants, shops and cultural institutions such as the contemporary art gallery The Arnolfini.

Birmingham is a major city in England’s West Midlands region, with multiple Industrial Revolution-era landmarks that speak to its 18th-century history as a manufacturing powerhouse. It’s also home to a network of canals, many of which radiate from Sherborne Wharf and are now lined with trendy cafes and bars. In the city centre, the Birmingham Museum and Art Gallery is known for pre-Raphaelite masterpieces.

Ghent is a port city in northwest Belgium, at the confluence of the Leie and Scheldt rivers. During the Middle Ages it was a prominent city-state. Today it’s a university town and cultural hub. Its pedestrianized center is known for medieval architecture such as 12th-century Gravensteen castle and the Graslei, a row of guildhalls beside the Leie river harbor.

Bruges is the capital of West Flanders in northwest Belgium, distinguished by its canals, cobbled streets and medieval buildings. Its port, Zeebrugge, is an important center for fishing and European trade. In the city center’s Burg square, the 14th-century Stadhuis (City Hall) has an ornate carved ceiling. Nearby, Markt square features a 13th-century belfry with a 47-bell carillon and an 83m tower with panoramic views.

Antwerp is a port city on Belgium’s River Scheldt, with history dating to the Middle Ages. In its center, the centuries-old Diamond District houses thousands of diamond traders, cutters and polishers. Antwerp’s Flemish Renaissance architecture is typified by the Grote Markt, a central square in the old town. At the 17th-century Rubens House, period rooms display works by the Flemish Baroque painter Peter Paul Rubens.

Rotterdam is a major port city in the Dutch province of South Holland. The Maritime Museum's vintage ships and exhibits trace the city's seafaring history. The 17th-century Delfshaven neighborhood is home to canalside shopping and the Pilgrim Fathers Church, where pilgrims worshiped before sailing to America. After being almost completely reconstructed following WWII, the city is now known for bold, modern architecture.

The Hague is a city on the North Sea coast of the western Netherlands. Its Gothic-style Binnenhof (or Inner Court) complex is the seat of the Dutch parliament, and 16th-century Noordeinde Palace is the king’s workplace. The city is also home to the U.N.’s International Court of Justice, headquartered in the Peace Palace, and the International Criminal Court. The 17th-century Mauritshuis art museum displays works by Dutch masters such as Vermeer and Rembrandt.

Gothenburg, a major city in Sweden, is situated off the Göta älv river on the country's west coast. An important seaport, it's known for its Dutch-style canals and leafy boulevards like the Avenyn, the city's main thoroughfare, lined with many cafes and shops. Liseberg is a popular amusement park with themed rides, performance venues and a landscaped sculpture garden.

Malmö is a coastal city in southern Sweden. It lies at the eastern end of the resund Bridge, a long road and railway bridge-tunnel running to Copenhagen, Denmark. In the city center, Lilla Torg is a cobblestone square with cafes, half-timbered houses and a fountain. Malmö Castle, a 16th-century fortress built by the Danes, is now a museum complex. The Turning Torso, a futuristic skyscraper, is a well-known landmark.

Bergen is a city on Norway’s southwestern coast. It's surrounded by mountains and fjords, including Sognefjord, the country’s longest and deepest. Bryggen, the old wharf, is a reminder of the city's importance as part of the Hanseatic League's trading empire. The Fløibanen Funicular goes up Fløyen Mountain for panoramic views and hiking trails.

Trondheim is a city on the Trondheim Fjord, in central Norway. Nidaros Cathedral, built over the burial site of King Olav II, is considered the most sacred building in Norway. The city's old harbor is lined with colorful wooden buildings. The nearby Gamle Bybro (Old Town Bridge) provides a great view of the harbor and the city. The Ringve Museum is the national museum for music and musical instruments.

Aarhus is a city in Denmark on the Jutland peninsula’s east coast. It's known for its university, vibrant music scene, and for the Old Town, an open-air museum of historic buildings from all over Denmark. The ARoS Aarhus Art Museum, with its circular skywalk, 'Your Rainbow Panorama', is a major landmark. The Moesgaard Museum is dedicated to prehistory and ethnography.

Porto is a coastal city in northwest Portugal known for its stately bridges and port wine production. In the medieval Ribeira (riverside) district, narrow cobbled streets wind past merchants’ houses and cafes. São Francisco Church is known for its lavish baroque interior with ornate gilded carvings. The palatial 19th-century Palácio de Bolsa, formerly a stock market, was built to impress potential European investors.

Faro is the capital of southern Portugal’s Algarve region. The city’s neoclassical Arco da Vila is on the site of a gate that was part of the original Moorish wall. The monumental archway leads to the old town, with its cobbled streets. Nearby is Faro Cathedral, built in the 13th century. The Municipal Museum, in a 16th-century convent, displays prehistoric and medieval artifacts, plus religious art.

Seville is the capital of southern Spain’s Andalusia region. It's famous for flamenco dancing, particularly in its Triana neighborhood. Major landmarks include the ornate Alcázar castle complex, built during the Moorish Almohad dynasty, and the 18th-century Plaza de Toros de la Maestranza bullring. The Gothic Seville Cathedral is the site of Christopher Columbus’s tomb and a minaret turned bell tower, the Giralda.

Valencia is the capital of the autonomous community of Valencia and the third-largest city in Spain. It is located on the east coast of Spain, on the Mediterranean Sea. The city is known for its City of Arts and Sciences, with futuristic structures including a planetarium, an oceanarium and an interactive museum. Valencia also has several beaches, including some within nearby Albufera Park, a wetlands reserve with a lake and walking trails.

Ibiza is one of the Balearic islands, an archipelago of Spain in the Mediterranean Sea. It's well known for the lively nightlife in Ibiza Town and Sant Antoni, where major European nightclubs have summer outposts. It’s also home to quiet villages, yoga retreats and beaches, from Platja d'en Bossa, lined with hotels, bars and shops, to quieter sandy coves backed by pine-clad hills found all around the coast.

Mallorca (Majorca) is one of Spain's Balearic Islands in the Mediterranean. It's known for beach resorts, sheltered coves, limestone mountains and Roman and Moorish remains. Capital Palma has a vibrant nightlife, the Moorish Almudaina royal palace and 13th-century Santa María Cathedral. Stone-built villages include Pollença, with its art galleries and music festival, and hillside Fornalutx, surrounded by citrus plantations.

Menorca is one of Spain’s Balearic Islands in the Mediterranean Sea. Traditionally more low-key than its neighbors, Mallorca (Majorca) and Ibiza, it’s known for its endless beaches, from miles-long sandy crescents to rocky, turquoise-watered bays called 'calas.' Pine trees fringe the coast. The capital, Mahón, is on a bluff overlooking a large harbor, with Georgian mansions and a church with 19th-century organ.

Milan, a metropolis in Italy's northern Lombardy region, is a global capital of fashion and design. Home to the national stock exchange, it’s a financial hub also known for its high-end restaurants and shops. The Gothic Duomo di Milano cathedral and the Santa Maria delle Grazie convent, housing Leonardo da Vinci’s mural 'The Last Supper,' testify to centuries of art and culture.

Naples, a city in southern Italy, sits on the Bay of Naples. Nearby is Mount Vesuvius, the still-active volcano that destroyed the nearby Roman town of Pompeii. Dating to the 2nd millennium B.C., Naples has centuries of important art and architecture. The city's cathedral, the Duomo di San Gennaro, is filled with frescoes. Other major landmarks include the lavish Royal Palace and Castel Nuovo, a 13th-century castle.

Venice, the capital of northern Italy’s Veneto region, is built on more than 100 small islands in a lagoon in the Adriatic Sea. It has no roads, just canals – including the Grand Canal thoroughfare – lined with Renaissance and Gothic palaces. The central square, Piazza San Marco, contains St. Mark’s Basilica, which is tiled with Byzantine mosaics, and the Campanile bell tower offering views of the city’s red roofs.

Cinque Terre is a string of centuries-old seaside villages on the rugged Italian Riviera coastline. In each of the 5 towns, colorful houses and vineyards cling to steep terraces, harbors are filled with fishing boats and trattorias turn out seafood specialties along with the Liguria region’s famous sauce, pesto. The Sentiero Azzurro cliffside hiking trail links the villages and offers sweeping sea vistas.

Pisa is a city in Italy’s Tuscany region best known for its iconic Leaning Tower. Already tilting when it was completed in 1372, the 56m white-marble cylinder is the bell tower of the Romanesque, striped-marble cathedral that rises next to it in the Piazza dei Miracoli. Also in the piazza is the Baptistry, whose renowned acoustics are demonstrated by amateur singers daily, and the Caposanto Monumentale cemetery.

Siena, a city in central Italy’s Tuscany region, is distinguished by its medieval brick buildings. The fan-shaped central square, Piazza del Campo, is the site of the Palazzo Pubblico, the Gothic town hall, and Torre del Mangia, a slender 14th-century tower with sweeping views from its distinctive white crown. The city’s 17 historic 'contrade' (districts) extend outward from the piazza.

Bologna is the lively, historic capital of the Emilia-Romagna region, in northern Italy. Its Piazza Maggiore is a sprawling plaza lined with arched colonnades, cafes and medieval and Renaissance structures such as City Hall, the Fountain of Neptune and the Basilica di San Petronio. Among the city’s many medieval towers are the two that dominate the skyline: the leaning Asinelli Tower and the Garisenda Tower.

Verona is a city in northern Italy’s Veneto region with a medieval old town built between the meandering Adige River. It’s famous for being the setting of Shakespeare’s 'Romeo and Juliet.' A 14th-century residence with a tiny balcony overlooking a courtyard is said be 'Juliet’s House.' The Verona Arena, a huge 1st-century Roman amphitheater, currently hosts concerts and large-scale opera performances.

Turin, the capital of Piedmont in northern Italy, is known for its refined architecture and cuisine. The Alps rise to the northwest of the city. Stately baroque buildings and old cafes line Turin's boulevards and grand squares such as Piazza Castello and Piazza San Carlo. Nearby is the soaring spire of the Mole Antonelliana, a 19th-century tower housing the interactive National Cinema Museum.

Genoa (Genova) is a port city and the capital of northwest Italy's Liguria region. It's known for its central role in maritime trade over many centuries. In the old town stands the Romanesque Cathedral of San Lorenzo, with its black-and-white-striped facade and frescoed interior. Narrow lanes open onto monumental squares like Piazza de Ferrari, site of an iconic bronze fountain and the Teatro Carlo Felice opera house.

Palermo is the capital of the Italian island of Sicily. The 12th-century Palermo Cathedral houses royal tombs, while the huge neoclassical Teatro Massimo is known for its opera performances. Also in the center are the Palazzo dei Normanni, a royal palace started in the 9th century, and the Cappella Palatina, with Byzantine mosaics. Busy markets include the central Ballarò street market and the Vucciria, near the port.

Catania is an ancient port city on Sicily's east coast. It sits at the foot of Mt. Etna, an active volcano with trails leading up to its summit. The city's wide central square, Piazza del Duomo, features the whimsical Fontana dell'Elefante statue and richly decorated Catania Cathedral. In the southwest corner of the square, La Pescheria weekday fish market is a rowdy spectacle surrounded by seafood restaurants.

Cagliari is the capital city of the Italian island of Sardinia. It’s known for its medieval walled quarter, Castello, which is situated high on a hill and offers panoramic views of the city. The National Archaeological Museum displays bronze objects, Roman ceramics and artifacts from the Nuragic age to the Byzantine era. The 13th-century Cagliari Cathedral features a baroque facade and a Gothic-style interior.
